metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.finding: Parent AC5 requires both `docs/releasing.md` AND `docs/migrations.md` to describe the store compatibility record (including the absent-record upgrade/rollback path). Child 21dd-7a0b-2119-4473 covers `docs/migrations.md` fully, but no child claims a section in `docs/releasing.md` for the store compatibility record. The three release stories (F1/F3/F4) each own named sections of `docs/releasing.md` ('Release-source authorization', 'Build-once', 'Pinning') but none includes store compatibility.
findings.location: child 21dd-7a0b-2119-4473 (scope/docs), child fd84-ba00-ba00-4f73 (docs/releasing.md scope), child 6168-df06-1e08-4ea3 (docs/releasing.md scope), child 08a8-b04e-ae59-4be4 (docs/releasing.md scope)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to child 21dd-7a0b-2119-4473's scope: 'docs/releasing.md — add a subsection (e.g. under a "Store compatibility" heading) describing the .store-compat.json record, the absent-record upgrade path, and the rollback-safe outcome for pre-v1.0 binaries.' Extend its AC to match.

findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Finding 5: add an explicit rollback/back-out path for users with removed config keys — either an expand-contract deprecation phase (warn-only in v1.0, fail-closed in v1.1) or a documented 'how to roll back' step (e.g., downgrade command + version pinning instructions), and surface that mechanism in the story description and AC.
findings.finding: Finding 5 introduces a hard nonzero exit for removed config keys (e.g., TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR, REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE, verify.require_verdict_for_close) that previously were silently ignored or produced only a generic warning. This is a user-visible breaking change, but the plan provides no explicit rollback/back-out mechanism or expand-contract (warn-then-fail) deprecation phase — only a runtime migration message. Users who have these keys set in deployed configs or scripts will hit a hard failure on upgrade with no way to temporarily suppress it while they migrate.
findings.location: Finding 5 description and Success Criteria (last bullet); AC (last bullet)
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) ship v1.0 with a WARN-only phase for the named tombstoned keys and schedule the hard-fail for v1.1 (classic expand-contract), or (b) keep the hard-fail in v1.0 but add an explicit AC line: 'docs/migrations.md lists each removed key, its replacement, and the minimum-effort rollback step (downgrade to <version> via <command>)' so users are not stranded.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a dedicated parent-level AC item for Finding 5 that verifies: removed high-impact inputs (e.g., `TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR`, `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE`, `verify.require_verdict_for_close`) emit a targeted migration message and exit nonzero, while genuinely unknown keys retain the existing forward-compatibility behavior.
findings.finding: Finding 5 (removed-input tombstones) has no dedicated Acceptance Criterion that verifies its specific deliverable; it is only implicitly covered by AC item 1 ('All five child stories are closed'), unlike Findings 1–4 which have at least one specific AC line each (AC items 3, 4, 5, or 6 reference their concrete outputs). This asymmetry means Finding 5's outcome cannot be independently gate-checked at the parent level.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section vs. Context (Finding 5)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assign AC6 ownership to one or more children (or the parent itself as a meta-task): each child ticket must carry an explicit Accept/Reject log of its plan-review advisory findings before its Gerrit votes are granted.
findings.finding: Parent AC6 — 'Each child story's plan-review advisory findings are recorded with an explicit Accept/Reject disposition and a one-line rationale (as a comment on the child ticket), and every Accepted finding is reflected in the child's description before that child's change receives its Gerrit LLM-Review +1 / Verified +1 votes' — is not covered by any child. No sibling defines a deliverable, AC item, or checklist step that owns this process requirement.
findings.location: Parent Acceptance Criteria AC6; no child in the sibling roster
findings.suggested_fix: Add to each child story an AC item of the form: '[ ] Plan-review advisory findings for this story are logged as a comment on this ticket with an explicit Accept/Reject disposition and one-line rationale; every Accepted finding is incorporated into this description before Gerrit votes are requested.' Alternatively, add a sixth coordination child that owns the disposition log for all five stories and is a dependency of the parent AC gate.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] fd84 extends test_release_workflow_pins.py to assert that neither the mcp_registry job nor the github_release job contains a github.ref_name or push:tags-conditional version derivation — the same parse-YAML approach used by 08a8's existing checks.
findings.finding: fd84 re-keys the mcp_registry and github_release jobs' version sources and trigger guards from the tag context (github.ref_name / push:tags if:) to the workflow_dispatch input, but no automated test asserts that no tag-based version derivation remains in those jobs after fd84 lands. The only verification stated is static/manual: 'workflow definition still containing those jobs and no github.ref_name/tag-if: version derivation remaining'.
findings.location: child fd84-ba00-ba00-4f73, Acceptance Criteria item 2; cross-reference child 08a8-b04e-ae59-4be4 (introduces mcp_registry), child 6168-df06-1e08-4ea3 (extends test_release_workflow_pins.py)
findings.suggested_fix: In fd84's scope, add to test_release_workflow_pins.py a check that: parses release.yml, locates the mcp_registry and github_release job blocks, and asserts no occurrence of 'github.ref_name' or "startsWith(github.ref, 'refs/tags/')" in those blocks' env/steps/if fields.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] 21dd clarifies the CI coverage test's actual scope in the AC: 'asserts that every known acquisition form (lock.acquire, write_lock, fsck_recover_cli, _apply_mutations) routes through the gate' — and adds a note that new write paths outside these forms require a manual gate-wiring step + a corresponding update to the grep patterns.
findings.finding: 21dd scopes the store-compatibility gate to lock.acquire() as the 'single convergence point', but the child's own What section acknowledges two paths that do NOT go through lock.acquire() (fsck_recover.py and the Jira outbound reconcile path) and addresses them with explicit per-site gates. However, the CI coverage test is specified to grep for 'lock.acquire(' and 'write_lock(' and 'fsck_recover_cli's + _apply_mutations's check_store_compat call' — it does NOT enumerate future ungated paths that might be added via new write primitives outside these three forms. If a future write path uses neither lock.acquire(), write_lock(), nor the two explicit per-site gates, the CI grep would not catch it. The child claims the grep 'asserts each routes through the gate — so a new un-gated write path in any acquisition form fails CI' but this is only true for the enumerated acquisition forms, creating a partial-without-remainder structural gap.
findings.location: child 21dd-7a0b-2119-4473, What section ('Completeness is CI-enforced'), Acceptance Criteria item 3 (coverage test/lint)
findings.suggested_fix: Amend the CI coverage test description to explicitly state it enforces 'all currently-known write path forms' rather than implying exhaustive coverage of all possible future write paths. Optionally, add a module-level WRITE_PATH_REGISTRY = [...] in the gate module that must be explicitly updated for new write primitives, making the incompleteness visible at code-review time.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the Success Criteria (or a linked definition), add the path, version-field name, and format of `server.json` so the lockstep assertion can be implemented and verified without code archaeology.
findings.finding: 'server.json' is referenced in the Success Criteria as a version-lockstep target ('the version input/pyproject/server.json') but is never defined — its location, schema, and role in the version assertion are unspecified. An executor unfamiliar with the repo cannot implement or verify the lockstep check without first investigating what server.json is.
findings.location: Success Criteria, first bullet: 'the version-lockstep across the version input/pyproject/server.json is asserted before build'
findings.suggested_fix: Append a parenthetical such as: '(server.json = `src/rebar/server.json`, `{"version": "<semver>"}`)' directly in the success criterion bullet, or add a one-line definition under the Context section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the retained policy for genuinely unknown config keys explicitly in the Success Criteria (e.g., 'emit the existing generic warning and continue — preserve-and-ignore') so the boundary with the fail-loud path is unambiguous and testable.
findings.finding: The Success Criteria for Finding 5 defers the forward-compatibility policy for genuinely unknown config keys to 'the project's chosen forward-compatibility policy' without stating what that policy is. An executor must infer the policy from existing code behavior rather than from the plan, creating a gap between what the plan specifies and what is actually implemented.
findings.location: Success Criteria, fifth bullet: 'genuinely unknown keys keep the project's chosen forward-compatibility policy'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'genuinely unknown keys keep the project's chosen forward-compatibility policy' with 'genuinely unknown keys emit a generic warning and continue (preserve-and-ignore), identical to today's behaviour'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assign the version-lockstep assertion (version input / pyproject / server.json) to a specific named Finding's child story and add a dedicated AC line that verifies it is wired into CI before the build step.
findings.finding: The Success Criteria introduces a 'version-lockstep assertion across the version input/pyproject/server.json' as a required deliverable, but this work is not attributed to any of the five named Findings in the Context section, has no dedicated Acceptance Criterion verifying it, and has no child story assigned to it — making it an orphan success criterion with no described owner or verifiable gate.
findings.location: Success Criteria (first bullet) vs. Context (Findings 1–5)
findings.suggested_fix: Attribute this deliverable to Finding 3's child story (build-once/artifact integrity), since that story already restructures the build pipeline, and add an AC line: 'Before the build step, CI asserts that the workflow version input, pyproject.toml version, and server.json version are identical; a mismatch exits nonzero.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Finding 5: assign an explicit documentation artifact (e.g., a 'Removed inputs' section in docs/migrations.md) that inventories each tombstoned key/env-var, its replacement or rationale, and the migration step, and add this to AC bullet 5 alongside the existing release and store-compatibility doc requirements.
findings.finding: Finding 5 introduces a new behavioral contract — a defined set of config keys/env vars that now produce a targeted hard-fail with migration guidance — but the plan does not assign any documentation artifact (section of docs/migrations.md, a new reference page, or an ADR) to record the tombstoned-key inventory, their replacements, and the migration path. The three release-related stories each have explicit doc-section ownership in docs/releasing.md, and Finding 2 is assigned docs/migrations.md, but Finding 5 has no parallel doc assignment.
findings.location: Sequencing section; AC bullet 5 ('docs/releasing.md and docs/migrations.md are updated…'); Finding 5 description
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC bullet 5: 'docs/migrations.md includes a "Removed inputs" section listing each tombstoned env-var and TOML key, its replacement (or explicit deprecation rationale), and the upgrade step.' Update the Sequencing paragraph to give Finding 5 ownership of that section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit empirical validation step for Finding 2: a RED integration-test fixture that exercises (a) absent-record → treated as v0/compatible + record auto-written, (b) present-but-incompatible → fail-closed on each wired path, (c) rollback scenario (record present, old binary) → store still operable; this fixture must be green before the Gerrit LLM-Review +1 is cast.
findings.finding: Finding 2's absent-record=legacy-compatible policy and ensure-registry idempotency across ALL wired paths (append/transition/claim/delete/archive, compaction, MCP writes, automatic repair, reconciler, Jira push) are asserted correct without any specified empirical validation step — no RED fixture, integration test, or spike is planned to confirm the upgrade-in-place and rollback semantics before this ships as a permanent v1.0 contract.
findings.location: Finding 2 description and Success Criteria (store-compatibility gate section)
findings.suggested_fix: In the Finding 2 child story, add a 'Validation' section: write a pytest integration fixture that creates a temp store, runs a covered write with no record (assert gate passes + record appears), then sets an incompatible version and asserts each mutation path raises before any side effect; run a third scenario with a v1.0 record against a stub 'old binary' (ignore unknown files) to confirm rollback safety. Make the fixture a required CI check before the story can receive Verified +1.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Finding 2 child story whether the compatibility gate is implemented as a single choke-point (e.g., a `with store.assert_compatible():` context manager or a write-gateway function all paths must call) or N explicit call sites, and if the latter, add a linter/grep CI check that asserts every public write entry-point invokes the gate.
findings.finding: Finding 2 wires a compatibility gate across 8+ distinct write paths with no specified choke-point interface or single enforcement layer — if each path calls the gate directly, future write paths (or new MCP tools) can silently omit the check with no compile-time or lint-time enforcement.
findings.location: Success Criteria: 'before any side effect across all store-mutation/publish paths (append/transition/claim/delete/archive, compaction, MCP writes, automatic repair, the reconciler inbound write path, and Jira push/reconcile)'
findings.suggested_fix: Introduce a single `StoreCompatibilityGuard` context manager (or a `@requires_compatible_store` decorator) that all write paths acquire; this makes omission a visible code-review gap rather than a silent runtime omission. Document the pattern in CLAUDE.md so future contributors know the convention.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to Finding 2's AC a requirement to record the store compatibility design (absent-record policy rationale, expand-contract migration contract, rollback invariants) in an ADR (e.g., `docs/adr/0001-store-format-versioning.md`) or a dedicated section of CLAUDE.md, not only in the user-facing migration guide.
findings.finding: Finding 2 introduces a novel architectural pattern — store format versioning with an expand-contract migration strategy, an absent-record legacy policy, and a rollback-safe upgrade path — but the plan only requires updates to user-facing docs (docs/releasing.md, docs/migrations.md); no ADR or CLAUDE.md update is required to capture the design rationale for future maintainers who must extend or evolve the format.
findings.location: AC bullet 5: 'docs/releasing.md and docs/migrations.md are updated … store compatibility record (including the absent-record upgrade/rollback path)'
findings.suggested_fix: Create docs/adr/0001-store-format-versioning.md covering: (1) why absent=v0/compatible rather than absent=blocked, (2) the expand-contract migration protocol future format bumps must follow, (3) the rollback invariant (record is opaque/ignored by old binaries). Reference it from docs/migrations.md.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit, documented recovery path for a corrupt/unreadable store-compatibility record (e.g. specify that deleting the record lets ensure-registry recreate it on next write, and add this to docs/migrations.md and the error message itself), and specify that the ensure-registry write is atomic (write-to-temp + rename) so interrupted writes do not produce a corrupt record.
findings.finding: The plan specifies fail-closed on a corrupt or unreadable store-compatibility record, but provides no recovery path for that state — leaving a user with no documented way to unblock their store without out-of-band intervention.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'corrupt/unreadable record' bullet; Finding 2 description
findings.suggested_fix: Require that the ensure-registry write use an atomic write (temp file + fsync + rename) to prevent partial writes from producing a corrupt record. Add one sentence to the Finding 2 AC and to docs/migrations.md: 'If the compatibility record is corrupt or unreadable, delete it; the next covered write will recreate it as the current format version.' Include this path in the error message emitted when fail-closing on a corrupt record.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] The Finding 1 child story must explicitly include updating the GitHub `pypi` environment's deployment branch/tag policy from `v*`-tags to `main`-branch-only (GitHub Settings → Environments → Deployment branches), not just changing the workflow trigger from tag-push to `workflow_dispatch`, so the environment gate and the workflow trigger are consistent.
findings.finding: The plan's Finding 1 mechanism depends on the GitHub `pypi` environment's deployment branch/tag policy being switched from its current `v*`-tags restriction (confirmed in docs/releasing.md: 'GitHub environment `pypi` exists, restricted to `v*` tags') to a `main`-branch-only policy. The plan says the environment is 're-keyed to `main`-via-dispatch' but does not make the environment-policy update a first-class required step. If an implementer correctly changes the workflow trigger (removing `push: tags: v*`, adding `workflow_dispatch`) but leaves the environment's `v*`-tag deployment-branch policy unchanged, GitHub will reject every dispatch-triggered publish job with an environment protection failure — breaking the mechanism entirely. Conversely, if the environment is accidentally left unrestricted, dispatch from non-`main` refs would be permitted, defeating the source-authorization goal.
findings.location: Success Criteria — Finding 1 paragraph: 'publication happens directly via OIDC trusted-publishing gated on a required-reviewers `pypi` environment re-keyed to `main`-via-dispatch'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Finding 1 story's Acceptance Criteria: 'The `pypi` GitHub environment's deployment branch/tag policy is changed from `Specific branches and tags: v*` to `Specific branches: main`', and document this step in `docs/releasing.md` under the one-time setup section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that modelcontextprotocol/registry publishes a verifiable checksum (e.g., SHA-256 sidecar asset or cosign attestation) alongside mcp-publisher binary releases, and document the specific digest-verification mechanism the Finding 4 child story will use.
findings.finding: The plan's Success Criteria asserts 'an exact-versioned + digest-verified MCP publisher' for Finding 4, but does not verify that modelcontextprotocol/registry publishes a checksum file (e.g., SHA-256 sidecar) alongside binary release tarballs that would enable the digest-verification step. The current release.yml fetches `releases/latest/download` with no version pin or digest check; the child story must resolve a specific, verifiable mechanism.
findings.location: Success Criteria (Finding 4 bullet): 'an exact-versioned + digest-verified MCP publisher'; .github/workflows/release.yml lines 128-129

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly assert the store's schema state AFTER rollback in Finding 2's story: specify (a) what the committed record looks like when a pre-v1.0 binary has written events to the store, (b) whether the v1.0 gate accepts that record on re-upgrade, and (c) if not, what the compensating-forward path is.
findings.finding: The plan asserts rollback safety by saying 'the record is an ignored-unknown file to old binaries,' but does not assert the store's schema STATE after rollback — specifically: (1) what the store looks like after a v1.0-written record is present and mutations occur under the old binary, and (2) whether a re-upgrade to v1.0 will correctly accept or re-gate that same record. The T11 rubric requires post-rollback schema state assertions, not merely that the old binary exits cleanly.
findings.location: Success Criteria section — 'a rollback to a pre-v1.0 binary leaves the store operable (the record is an ignored-unknown file to old binaries)'; AC bullet 'The Finding 2 story defines and documents … a rollback-safe outcome for a pre-v1.0 binary encountering the record'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a subsection to Finding 2's story covering the rollback window: state that the record's format-version is immutable once written, that all mutations by pre-v1.0 binaries during the rollback window are by definition v0-compatible (since the gate didn't exist), and that v1.0 on re-upgrade sees a valid v1.0 record and proceeds normally. If this is not guaranteed by the record design, specify the compensating-forward step (e.g., `rebar fsck --repair` re-validates and if needed rewrites the record).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define and document a recovery path for a corrupt or unreadable compatibility record in Finding 2's story — e.g., `rebar fsck --repair` detects and rewrites a corrupt record, or a documented manual step to delete/repair the file, with a targeted error message pointing to the recovery procedure.
findings.finding: The plan specifies fail-closed on a corrupt or unreadable compatibility record, but provides no recovery (escape-hatch) path for that state. A user whose record becomes corrupt — e.g., from a disk error, a git merge conflict left unresolved, or a manual edit — is permanently locked out of all store write paths with no documented way to unblock themselves. Reads and diagnostics are allowed, but this is a partial-failure state with no back-out or compensating-forward path described.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'fails closed … or a corrupt/unreadable record, before any side effect across all store-mutation/publish paths'; AC bullet about fail-closed behaviour
findings.suggested_fix: Wire the corrupt-record case into `rebar fsck --repair`: if the record is present but unreadable/corrupt, `--repair` should rewrite it to the current format version (equivalent to a fresh absent-record write). The fail-closed error message should include a pointer: 'Run `rebar fsck --repair` to recover.' This satisfies T11(e) by providing a concrete back-out/compensating-forward path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that the modelcontextprotocol/registry releases page exposes a checksums file or equivalent digest manifest for the mcp-publisher binary, and document the exact URL/mechanism in the Finding 4 child story; if no digest is available, specify the fallback pinning strategy (e.g., exact-version URL without hash, or a SHA-pinned Action wrapper).
findings.finding: The plan's Success Criteria (Finding 4) assert 'an exact-versioned + digest-verified MCP publisher,' but the current implementation downloads mcp-publisher from `releases/latest` without a version pin or hash verification, and neither the plan nor the codebase shows how a per-binary checksum would be obtained — the modelcontextprotocol/registry releases page does not visibly provide a checksums/SBOM file alongside the tarball assets, making digest-verification of this specific binary an unverified capability.
findings.location: Success Criteria / `.github/workflows/release.yml` lines 127-130 ('Install mcp-publisher' step)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in Finding 4's child story that the mcp_registry job's digest verification of the mcp-publisher binary is a **sequential blocking step** (`sha256sum --check` exit-gated before `./mcp-publisher login`) within the `id-token: write` job, not an inline annotation in the download pipe, so a hash mismatch aborts the job before the OIDC token is ever presented to the binary.
findings.finding: The mcp_registry job holds `id-token: write` and — even after Finding 4's exact-version fix — downloads and immediately executes mcp-publisher in the same privileged job context with no isolation between the hash-verification step and the OIDC token mint; the plan's AC says 'exact-versioned + digest-verified MCP publisher' but does not require that digest verification is a sequential blocking gate enforced before execution, so a compromised release artifact or a TOCTOU on the resolved URL could still capture the short-lived OIDC token before verification fires.
findings.location: Finding 4 / Success Criteria ('exact-versioned + digest-verified MCP publisher') and release.yml mcp_registry job, lines 117-134
findings.suggested_fix: In the mcp_registry job, separate download and verification into two distinct run steps with `set -e`: step 1 downloads the tarball to a named file and writes/checks its SHA-256 against a pinned digest (`echo '<digest>  mcp-publisher.tar.gz' | sha256sum -c`); step 2 extracts and executes only if step 1 exits 0. This ensures the OIDC token is never passed to an unverified binary.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] 08a8 adds documentation (Makefile help text or docs/developing.md) explaining how to install actionlint locally, and the Makefile prerequisite target is idempotent (skips download if the pinned binary is already present at the expected path and its SHA-256 matches).
findings.finding: 08a8 installs actionlint via a Makefile prerequisite (pinned-version GitHub release download + SHA-256), but the story does not fully specify how this prerequisite integrates for developer-local environments. Developers running `pip install -e '.[dev]'` get zizmor but not actionlint; `make lint` triggers the Makefile prerequisite, but if the prerequisite target is not idempotent or requires network access unavailable in air-gapped environments, `make lint` silently degrades or fails for developers outside CI.
findings.location: child 08a8-b04e-ae59-4be4, Scope and Acceptance Criteria (actionlint install via Makefile prerequisite); consumer: developers running make lint locally
findings.suggested_fix: Add a Makefile target (e.g. `install-dev-tools`) that downloads and verifies actionlint idempotently, invoked as a prerequisite of `make lint`. Document it in the repo's development setup instructions. Consider also adding a graceful 'actionlint not found — run make install-dev-tools' error message in the lint target rather than a raw 'command not found'.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
